title: Table of Content
description: Table of Content scans a target container, builds hierarchical in-page navigation, tracks the active section, and supports sticky placement, heading filtering, and custom heading selectors.
intro: Use `ui-table-of-content` in documentation pages, long settings panels, knowledge content, or any reading surface where users benefit from quick in-page navigation. The real quality of the result depends more on content structure than on visual styling.

featureSections.title: Basic in-page navigation
featureSections.description: The baseline pattern is a TOC rail beside a content area with semantic headings. This is the main use case and should stay simple.

featureSections.title: Heading level filtering
featureSections.description: Min and max heading levels help limit noise. Use them when the content has deeper internal structure than the navigation should expose.

featureSections.title: Sticky behavior and offset
featureSections.description: Sticky placement keeps the current outline visible while the content scrolls. Offset is important when the page has a fixed header or another sticky shell.

featureSections.title: Appearance and indicator style
featureSections.description: Appearance changes how strong the navigation feels. Keep the TOC quieter than the content unless it is a primary side rail.

featureSections.title: Custom heading selectors
featureSections.description: You can build the outline from custom markers instead of heading tags. Use this when content is rendered from richer CMS or component-driven structures.

featureSections.title: Documentation layout composition
featureSections.description: TOC usually lives inside a broader documentation layout with callouts, dividers, and richer section content. This pattern is what users actually consume.

accessibility.title: Navigation semantics
accessibility.body: `ui-table-of-content` renders a navigation region and internally maps its entries to tree-node based navigation items. The content area it indexes should still use meaningful heading structure.

accessibility.title: Keyboard behavior
accessibility.body:
Keyboard interaction follows the tree-node based row model used inside the component.

| Key | Action |
| --- | --- |
| Tab / Shift+Tab | Move focus into, through, and out of the table of contents. |
| Enter / Space | Activate the focused entry and scroll to the linked section. |
| ArrowRight / ArrowLeft | Expand or collapse nested TOC groups when deeper levels are present. |

accessibility.title: Heading structure
accessibility.body: The TOC can only be as accessible and understandable as the source content. Use clear heading text, avoid skipping levels without reason, and prefer semantic headings over custom selectors when possible.

apiSections.title: TableOfContentComponent - ui-table-of-content
apiSections.lead: Standalone component that scans a target container, builds a hierarchical outline from headings, tracks the active section while scrolling, and renders it as a navigable in-page tree.

blocks.title: Inputs
rows: CSS selector for the content container to scan.
Selector used to collect source headings or custom markers.
Makes the TOC stick within its layout column.
Top offset used for sticky positioning and scroll targeting.

blocks.title: Models
rows: Internal outline item generated from source headings.

paragraphs: Prefer semantic headings as the source of truth. Custom selectors are useful, but they should be the exception rather than the default pattern.
If the page has many deeply nested headings, do not expose all of them automatically. Constrain the visible levels so the navigation stays readable.
blocks.title: Notes
